title: Unit Final: Credit, Debt, and Loans

choices.text: Credit is the ability to borrow money and repay it later
choices.feedback: Correct. Credit gives access to borrowed money that must later be repaid.

choices.text: Credit is money that never has to be repaid
choices.feedback: Borrowed money must still be repaid.

choices.text: Credit is only the money already saved in an account
choices.feedback: Credit is about borrowing, not simply saving.

choices.text: Credit is a fee attached to all purchases
choices.feedback: Credit is not a fee.

questions.prompt: A borrower compares two $500 loan options. Option A adds $45 interest and a $10 fee. Option B adds $30 interest and a $5 fee. Which option has the lower total repayment?

choices.text: Option A
choices.feedback: Add the full borrowing costs for both options before deciding.

choices.text: Option B
choices.feedback: Correct. Option A totals $555, while Option B totals $535, so Option B costs less.

choices.text: They cost the same because both borrow $500
choices.feedback: The borrowed amount is the same, but the interest and fees are different.

choices.text: It cannot be compared because the interest amounts are different
choices.feedback: Different interest amounts are exactly why the options should be compared.

questions.prompt: Making payments on time is generally a stronger credit behavior than missing due dates.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Correct. On-time payment behavior is generally stronger for future borrowing.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: Missing due dates is usually a weaker credit behavior.

questions.prompt: Which borrowing option is most closely connected to education expenses?

choices.text: Mortgage
choices.feedback: Mortgages are associated with property purchases.

choices.text: Credit card
choices.feedback: A credit card is not the borrowing option most closely connected to education expenses.

choices.text: Student loan
choices.feedback: Correct. Student loans are linked to education-related borrowing.

choices.text: Sales tax
choices.feedback: Sales tax is not a borrowing option.

questions.prompt: Why is total repayment more useful than the borrowed amount alone when comparing loans?

choices.text: Because the borrowed amount is the only number that matters
choices.feedback: The borrowed amount alone does not show the full cost. Interest and fees matter too.

choices.text: Because all loans cost the same after repayment
choices.feedback: Loans can cost different amounts depending on added borrowing costs.

choices.text: Because total repayment includes interest and fees
choices.feedback: Correct. Strong comparisons use the full repayment amount, not only the starting amount.

choices.text: Because fees and interest never affect borrowing decisions
choices.feedback: Fees and interest are key parts of borrowing decisions.

questions.prompt: Scenario: A borrower needs money for a home purchase. Which option is most closely matched to that purpose?

choices.text: Auto loan
choices.feedback: An auto loan is not the borrowing option most closely matched to a home purchase.

choices.text: Credit card for any amount needed
choices.feedback: A credit card is not the strongest borrowing tool for a home purchase.

choices.text: Student loan
choices.feedback: Student loans are associated with education costs.

choices.text: Mortgage
choices.feedback: Correct. A mortgage is most closely associated with borrowing for a home.

questions.prompt: Borrowing again to cover older debt can increase long-term financial risk.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: Using new debt to cover older debt can increase financial risk over time.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Correct. That pattern can make debt harder to manage.

questions.prompt: Scenario: Option A borrows $700 with $63 interest and a $12 fee. Option B borrows $700 with $45 interest and a $6 fee. What is the strongest recommendation?

choices.text: Choose Option A because both options borrow the same amount
choices.feedback: The borrowed amount is the same, so the stronger comparison is the full repayment cost.

choices.text: Choose Option B because its monthly payment might feel easier even if the total cost mattered less
choices.feedback: That does not use the strongest comparison. The better recommendation is based on the lower total repayment.

choices.text: Choose Option B because its total repayment is lower
choices.feedback: Correct. Option A totals $775, and Option B totals $751, so Option B is stronger.

choices.text: The two options cannot be compared because both use interest
choices.feedback: They can be compared by calculating total repayment.

questions.prompt: Scenario: Borrower A follows the repayment schedule each month. Borrower B skips payments and adds new debt to cover old debt. Which conclusion is strongest?

choices.text: Borrower B is showing stronger debt management because adding more debt solves the problem
choices.feedback: Adding new debt to cover old debt is not stronger debt management.

choices.text: Borrower B is showing stronger debt management because more borrowing adds flexibility
choices.feedback: Adding new debt to cover old debt can increase long-term risk.

choices.text: Debt management does not depend on repayment behavior
choices.feedback: Repayment behavior is a major part of debt management.

choices.text: Borrower A is showing stronger debt management
choices.feedback: Correct. Following a repayment plan is the stronger long-term strategy.

questions.prompt: Scenario: A borrower is approved for a loan but has no realistic plan to repay it. What is the strongest judgment?

choices.text: The loan is automatically a strong choice because it was approved
choices.feedback: Approval alone does not mean the debt is manageable.

choices.text: The stronger choice is to compare repayment ability before accepting the debt
choices.feedback: Correct. Responsible borrowing requires checking whether repayment is realistic before taking the debt.

choices.text: Repayment planning only matters after borrowing is complete
choices.feedback: Repayment planning matters before borrowing as well as after.

choices.text: Long-term consequences should be ignored if the money is needed right away
choices.feedback: Strong borrowing decisions should consider long-term consequences.
